KSQL → KBDN

12,000 ft
Departure 2026-03-30 14:00Z Arrival 2026-03-30 16:20Z Route 398 nm Charts 13 Generated 2026-03-28 16:49Z
Executive Summary

Executive Summary

This is a developing Pacific system day — a deepening low and associated cold front are tracking into the Pacific Northwest on Monday, putting the Pacific Coast and Cascades under an active weather regime by your planned departure time. Primary Risk: The frontal system will be driving significant precipitation, gusty and potentially strong winds over terrain, and deteriorating ceilings into and around the Cascade region including KBDN by the 14:00–16:20Z window. MARGINAL GO at best, trending toward NO-GO depending on how the front positions Monday morning. Single most important trigger: The position and timing of the cold front over Oregon at Monday 12Z — if it reaches the Cascades before your arrival, KBDN likely goes below VFR minimums with gusty crosswinds; if it's still north of the Oregon border at departure, the flight is workable.

Area Forecast Discussion — Aviation
AFD Aviation Sections 2 WFOs
WFO MTR Departure
.AVIATION...
(12Z TAFS)
Issued at 439 AM PDT Sat Mar 28 2026

Stratus is making its way up the coastline with HAF reporting
ceilings around 500 ft. Kept in fog chances for STS but it is
looking less likely for other airports tonight. Stratus is arriving
later this morning than originally forecast but it should fill in
along the coastline within the next few hours. Guidance continues to
indicate clearing by 17-19Z but there is some potential for stratus
to last longer at coastal sites (HAF, MRY, SNS). A shallow marine
layer looks to develop again tonight which will bring stratus back
to coastal sites. Confidence is mixed as to the timing of tonight`s
stratus return. Guidance is favoring a later return of stratus
(after 06Z) compared to an early return (00-06Z) like was in the 06Z
TAFs. Given the later return of stratus tonight, I tended to agree
with the later stratus arrival tomorrow night but this may need to
be adjusted.

Vicinity of SFO...VFR with a low potential for stratus to reach SFO
this morning. Confidence is highest that the shallow marine layer
(~500 ft) will keep stratus confined directly to the coastline and
won`t filter into the SF Bay. Breezy NW winds continue through this
afternoon before winds ease this evening/overnight. Low chance for
stratus to reach SFO again tonight but confidence is low given that
the marine layer is forecast to be shallow and around 500 ft again.

SFO Bridge Approach...Similar to SFO.

Monterey Bay Terminals...Stratus is filtering into the Monterey Bay
and Salinas Valley. IFR ceilings are expected to reach SNS by 12/13Z
and last through the late morning. Ceilings will take slightly
longer to fill in at MRY but will at least temporarily arrive
between 13-17Z. Moderate confidence that ceilings will clear 17/18Z
but there is some potential for ceilings to last longer than in the
current forecast. HREF guidance shows stratus hanging around along
the coastline through this afternoon. If the stratus bank does not
dissipate much during the day then we may see an earlier return of
stratus tonight. For now leaned towards a later arrival of stratus
but this may need to be adjusted.

Operational Briefing

The Day Before — What to Watch

  • Front timing: The 48-hr prog (valid Mon 12Z) places a deepening low and cold front squarely over the Pacific Northwest. Check Sunday evening model runs for whether the front accelerates or slows — even 3–4 hours of timing shift changes this from marginal to no-go.
  • KBDN winds: The PDT AFD already calls for winds 12–20 kt gusting 20–30 kt at RDM/BDN between 19Z and 03Z Sunday night. Check whether Monday's TAFs maintain or amplify this. Winds >25 kt gusting >35 kt at KBDN with a significant crosswind component is a hard stop.
  • Ceilings at KBDN: If Monday TAFs show BKN/OVC below 3,000 ft AGL by 14:00–16:00Z, the arrival picture degrades significantly. KBDN has no instrument approach capable of getting you down in low IFR — check current approach minima and alternates.
  • QPF at KBDN: The Day2 42–48hr QPF shows precipitation reaching the Idaho/Montana border and central Oregon by Mon 06Z. If the rain band is already over Bend by your ETA, expect low ceilings and possible IMC on approach.
  • KSQL marine layer: MTR WFO flags coastal stratus with confidence issues on timing. If stratus is still in the Bay at 14:00Z Monday, you may need a VFR hold before departure — minor issue compared to the arrival end.
  • Watch for: Any SIGMET for mountain wave or severe turbulence over the Oregon Cascades. With a deepening low, wave activity is a real possibility at 12,000 ft.

Leaving KSQL — Departure at 2026-03-30 14:00Z

TAFs for KSQL are not yet valid for the Monday 14:00Z window, so treat this as low confidence on specifics. However, the 48-hr surface prog and MTR AFD guidance both indicate the Pacific front is the story up north, not at the Bay Area. At 14:00Z Monday:

  • The Bay Area should be behind any marine layer episode, with the typical diurnal clearing trend well underway by that hour (marine layer typically clears SFO area by 17–19Z per the AFD, but Monday's system may alter this).
  • Most likely departure scenario: VMC or low-VFR conditions at KSQL with broken coastal stratus burning off. Ceilings 1,500–3,500 ft, visibility 5+ miles once clear of the immediate coastal band.
  • Worst-case departure: If an earlier-than-forecast marine layer return occurs, you could see 500–1,000 ft ceilings at KSQL. At 14:00Z (7 AM local), this is possible but not the most likely scenario.
  • Runway 30 departure is standard at KSQL in non-calm conditions. Note the 100 ft transmission towers 750 ft west and parallel to the runway — keep your climbout on centerline. The 7 ft lighted dike at the approach end of Runway 30 is behind you on departure; the dike on the approach end of Runway 12 is ahead — not a factor for Runway 30 departure.
  • Bottom line departure: Likely VMC departure with possible brief coastal layer to climb through. The departure end is not the problem on this flight.

The Ride at 12,000 ft

Winds Aloft

No winds aloft data was provided for this briefing. Based on the synoptic pattern from the surface progs, I can characterize the flow directionally:

  • A deepening low is positioned off the Pacific Northwest coast by Sunday evening, deepening further by Monday. This drives a southwesterly to westerly flow aloft over the California/Oregon corridor.
  • At 12,000 ft MSL over the Oregon Cascades on Monday, expect westerly to southwesterly winds of 25–45 kt based on the tight pressure gradient shown in the 48-hr prog. This is a rough estimate — pull actual winds-aloft forecast (FD) data for the flight-planned altitude before departure.
  • For KSQL→KBDN (roughly north-northeast track), westerly winds at cruise altitude translate to a left crosswind with a possible tailwind component. Rough estimate: 10–20 kt headwind component depending on exact wind direction. Do not count on a fast trip.

Action required: Obtain FD winds for OAK, RDD, MFR, or equivalent stations along the route. Do not depart without actual winds-aloft data given the frontal gradient.

Ride Quality

RegionExpected RideBasis
KSQL to Sacramento Valley (first ~150 nm)Smooth to light chopHigh pressure ridge still dominant, light surface gradient
Northern California / Shasta areaLight to moderate turbulence possibleApproaching frontal trough, increasing flow over terrain
Oregon Cascades crossing (last ~80 nm to KBDN)Moderate turbulence likelyDeepening low driving strong westerly flow over high terrain; mountain wave possible
Bend arrivalLight to moderate turbulenceTerrain-induced rotor zone on lee side of Cascades; wind gradient near surface

Wind-over-terrain note: With estimated 30–45 kt flow at altitude over the Oregon Cascades (peaks 6,000–9,000+ ft MSL), mountain wave is a likely factor. At 12,000 ft you may be in the wave itself or in rotor zone turbulence depending on wavelength. This is the most significant airborne hazard on this route.

Icing

No FIP icing data was provided. However, the synoptic setup demands attention:

Altitude BandIcing ProbabilityNotes
Surface – 6,000 ft MSLLow (over Bay Area)Marine stratus, temps above freezing at low levels
6,000 – 10,000 ft MSLPossible (conditional)Frontal moisture approaching Oregon; temps near or below freezing in clouds at this band over the Cascades
10,000 – 12,000 ft MSLPossible (conditional)At 12,000 ft over Oregon Cascades, temps likely subfreezing with frontal cloud deck moving in; icing possible if in cloud
Above 12,000 ftLow (above cloud tops)If you can get above the frontal deck — possible only if tops are lower than 12,000 ft, which is uncertain
  • Freezing level: March in Oregon Cascades — estimate 6,000–8,000 ft MSL near the front. Confirm with actual freezing level forecast before flight.
  • Worst icing band: 8,000–12,000 ft over the Oregon Cascades if frontal clouds are already in place at your ETA.
  • SLD risk: No SLD signal visible in the data provided. With a maritime Pacific frontal system, SLD cannot be ruled out in the 8,000–12,000 ft band near the frontal zone. Treat as possible, low confidence.
  • If not certified for known icing: This is a significant concern for the Oregon Cascade crossing. A non-FIKI aircraft has limited escape options in this terrain band.

Altitude Strategy

  • Best altitude band: 10,000–12,000 ft if you can stay above the cloud layer and the front hasn't fully arrived. This keeps you clear of the lower rotor zone and most surface-driven turbulence.
  • Worst altitude band: 8,000–10,000 ft over the Cascades — likely in the icing zone and in moderate turbulence from terrain interaction with the frontal flow.
  • Escape option: If icing or turbulence is encountered over the Cascades, your primary escape is a 180-degree turn back toward Redding (KRDD) or Klamath Falls (KLMT). Do not attempt to press into deteriorating conditions in the Cascades with limited visibility or significant icing. Descend is not a reliable option if terrain rises above your safe altitude. Divert is the right call.

Hazard Table

HazardRiskLegWhat to Expect
Mountain wave / terrain turbulenceLikelyOregon Cascades, final 80 nmModerate turbulence; possible severe in wave rotor zone. Strong westerly flow over 6,000–9,000 ft terrain.
Frontal icingPossibleNorthern CA / Oregon CascadesStructural icing in cloud at 8,000–12,000 ft if frontal deck has arrived. Confirm freezing level before departure.
Low ceilings / IMC at KBDNPossible to Likely depending on front timingArrivalFrontal cloud and precipitation moving through Bend. Ceilings may be below VFR minimums by your 16:20Z ETA.
Strong surface winds at KBDNLikelyArrivalPDT AFD calls for 12–20 kt gusting 20–30 kt. Crosswind component depends on wind direction vs. Runway 16/34.

ConvectionLowEn routeNo convective signal along the route for this time window.

Getting Into KBDN

KBDN is a non-towered airport at 3,459 ft MSL in the high desert east of the Cascades. The Cascades block most of the direct Pacific moisture, but with a deepening, well-organized Pacific low, the frontal band will push precipitation and cloud east of the Cascades. The 48-hr surface prog (valid Mon 12Z) and the QPF Day2 42–48hr product both show precipitation reaching into central Oregon by Monday morning.

  • Expected arrival conditions (16:20Z Monday): This is the high-risk window. The front is likely making landfall or crossing the Cascades around this time. Best case: scattered clouds 3,000–5,000 ft AGL, winds 15–25 kt gusty from the southwest, VFR. Worst case: BKN/OVC 1,500–2,500 ft, rain, winds 20–30 kt gusting, marginal VFR to low IFR.
  • Runway wind estimate: KBDN Runway 16/34 is aligned roughly N-S. Southwesterly frontal winds of 15–25 kt would produce a significant crosswind component on both runways — estimate 10–20 kt crosswind. High uncertainty on exact wind direction at surface; terrain channeling east of the Cascades can redirect winds significantly from the synoptic flow. Do not assume surface winds match winds aloft.
  • Practical alternates: KRDM (Redmond, OR) is 16 nm north of Bend, has instrument approaches, a longer runway (7,004 ft), and is towered. It is your go-to alternate if KBDN is below minimums. KLMT (Klamath Falls, OR) is farther (~80 nm SW) but is an option if the system is pushing east faster than forecast. Ensure you have fuel for at least KBDN + KRDM + 45 minutes reserve.
  • Estimated block time: KSQL to KBDN is approximately 2 hours 20 minutes at cruise with neutral winds. With a 10–20 kt headwind component, plan 2:30–2:45 block time. Departure 14:00Z = arrival approximately 16:30–16:45Z. That's squarely in the frontal arrival window.
  • Non-towered ops note: KBDN is non-towered with a listed operating hours of 0800–1700 local (16:00–01:00Z in PDT/PST — confirm UTC offset). Your 16:20Z ETA is at or just after local opening; be prepared for self-announce traffic and confirm CTAF frequency.

Fly or No

MARGINAL GO — trending toward NO-GO as front timing clarifies.

The current data places a deepening Pacific low and cold front over the Pacific Northwest precisely during your planned flight window. The Bay Area departure is likely fine. The problems are at the arrival end and en route over the Cascades.

Hard stops:

  • KBDN TAF (once issued) shows BKN or OVC below 1,500 ft AGL or visibility below 3 miles at ETA — NO-GO.
  • Surface winds at KBDN forecast gusting above 35 kt — NO-GO (runway length at 3,459 ft density altitude with significant crosswind is not forgiving).
  • Aircraft is not certified for known icing and PIREPs/forecasts indicate moderate icing at 12,000 ft over the Cascades — NO-GO.
  • SIGMET issued for severe turbulence over the Oregon Cascades — NO-GO.

Watch items (MARGINAL conditions — proceed with caution):

  • KBDN forecast ceiling 1,500–3,000 ft AGL at ETA with winds 15–25 kt gusty: Marginal, go only with KRDM as filed alternate and extra fuel.
  • G-AIRMET for moderate mountain obscuration or turbulence over Oregon Cascades: Reassess altitude strategy; file IFR and have escape plan briefed.

What Changes This Call

  • Front timing shifts 6+ hours earlier: If Monday morning progs show the cold front crossing the Cascades before 14:00Z, KBDN is likely below VFR at your ETA. This is a NO-GO.
  • KBDN TAF (once issued) shows ceilings below 2,000 ft AGL or gusts above 30 kt at ETA: NO-GO.
  • Monday morning PIREPs over the Oregon Cascades report moderate or greater turbulence or icing: NO-GO or major altitude/route revision required.
  • Front timing shifts 6+ hours later than forecast: If Sunday evening models push the front to arrive at KBDN after 20:00Z Monday, the window opens — MARGINAL GO becomes a more confident GO with KRDM as alternate.

If You Go — Do This

  1. Morning-of briefing (Monday, 06:00–08:00Z): Pull updated 12Z surface prog, any new TAFs for KBDN and KRDM, winds aloft FD for OAK-MFR-RDD corridor at 12,000 ft, current G-AIRMETs, and any SIGMETs for the Pacific Northwest. This is not optional — the frontal timing question is unresolved and only the morning-of data resolves it.
  2. Altitude: Plan 12,000 ft initially but be ready to go to 10,000 ft if tops are above you and you need to stay VFR, or climb to 14,000 ft if you can get above the frontal deck (oxygen required). Do not attempt to fly through frontal IMC at 12,000 ft over the Cascades in a non-IFR/non-FIKI setup.
  3. File IFR if instrument rated and aircraft equipped: The terrain complexity and frontal system make this a route where IFR provides meaningful safety margin even if VFR is technically legal at departure. An IFR clearance also gives you options if you encounter IMC unexpectedly over the Cascades.
  4. Alternates and fuel: File KRDM as your alternate. Carry fuel for KSQL–KBDN + KBDN–KRDM + 45 minutes. That's approximately 3.5–4 hours total endurance minimum.
  5. Brief your abort point: If you are not VFR and comfortable at the northern California/Oregon border (approximately Klamath Falls area), turn around. Do not press into the Cascades with deteriorating conditions ahead. Redding (KRDD) and Klamath Falls (KLMT) are your turnback options.
  6. Check density altitude at KBDN: 3,459 ft MSL field elevation, potential gusty winds, wet runway — brief your landing performance accordingly.

Timing note: A later departure (16:00–17:00Z instead of 14:00Z) does not help here — it puts you at KBDN even later into the frontal arrival window. If anything, an earlier departure (12:00Z Monday, allowing earlier arrival) might keep you ahead of the worst of the front, but only if the marine layer departure constraint is resolved. Evaluate this trade-off with morning data.

Confidence

Low-to-Medium overall. Low specifically for KBDN arrival conditions.

  • This is a 36–48 hour forecast window for the critical part of the flight — that's inherently moderate confidence territory, and this particular system involves a deepening Pacific low where timing errors of 3–6 hours are common.
  • The PDT WFO AFD confirms awareness of the wind event for Sunday night but does not yet address Monday specifics for KBDN — the TAF window hasn't opened.
  • No winds aloft, FIP icing, GTG turbulence, or PIREP data was available for this briefing. All three are essential for this route and must be obtained before any go decision.
  • The mountain wave potential over the Oregon Cascades is real and cannot be fully characterized without turbulence forecast products and PIREPs.
  • Confidence increases significantly with Monday morning data: A fresh 00Z or 06Z analysis with updated TAFs and PIREPs will sharpen this from MARGINAL to either GO or NO-GO with much higher certainty.
